messages.content:
You are a GDPR compliance analyst. When given a document excerpt, evaluate it step by step against each GDPR requirement. For each step, quote the relevant text if found, or state it is missing. End with a structured verdict listing all violations. Always follow this exact format:

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): ...
Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): ...
Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): ...
Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): ...
Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): ...
Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): ...
Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): ...
Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): ...
Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): ...
Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): ...

VERDICT: [Compliant / Non-Compliant / Partially Compliant]
VIOLATIONS: [list each article breached]
RECOMMENDATIONS: [one line per fix needed]

Assess the following food delivery platform privacy policy for GDPR compliance:

"HungerDash processes your name, delivery address, phone number, payment details, and order history to deliver food orders. We process this under our contract with you. Payments are handled by Adyen (EU-based, DPA signed). We use your order history to recommend restaurants — this processing is based on our legitimate interest in improving your experience. We have conducted a Legitimate Interest Assessment for this purpose. Order data is retained for 3 years; payment records for 7 years per financial regulations. You have the right to access, correct, erase, port, restrict, and object to your data. To exercise any right, contact privacy@hungerdash.com. Our DPO is at dpo@hungerdash.com. Data is stored in EU data centres. We notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours of a confirmed breach and customers without undue delay."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PASS] Contract performance (Art. 6(1)(b)) for order delivery. Legitimate interest (Art. 6(1)(f)) with documented LIA for recommendations. Financial legal obligation (Art. 6(1)(c)) implied for 7-year payment retention. All bases correctly applied and separated.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PARTIAL] Order history may reveal dietary requirements related to religion or health. No explicit safeguard.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Order fulfilment, personalised recommendations, and financial compliance are each stated as distinct purposes with separate lawful bases.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PASS] Data collected is standard and proportionate.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] Order data: 3 years. Payment records: 7 years. Both stated and legally grounded.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PASS] Access (Art. 15), rectification (Art. 16), erasure (Art. 17), portability (Art. 20), restriction (Art. 18), and objection (Art. 21) all listed with a contact mechanism.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PASS] Adyen named as EU-based payment processor with signed DPA.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [PASS] EU data centres confirmed.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [PASS] DPO contact at dpo@hungerdash.com disclosed.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [PASS] 72-hour supervisory authority notification and customer notification without undue delay both stated.

VERDICT: Compliant
VIOLATIONS: None
RECOMMENDATIONS: No changes required. Consider acknowledging that order history may reveal dietary preferences linked to religion or health, and confirming this is handled appropriately within the LIA.
